title: Prep for a needs assessment call
teaser: Because "good conversation" is not a deliverable.
intro: You've got a learning needs assessment call on the calendar. The temptation is to walk in lightly prepped and "see where the conversation goes." Depending on the stakeholder, that can go really well, or really bad.
why_matters: A needs assessment is the meeting that shapes everything you do next. If you walk in without a clear idea of what you actually need by the end, you'll have a nice chat and then realize, back at your desk, you can't actually design from your notes. Then you're either booking another call or guessing your way through.
how_ai_helps: AI can help you get smarter about a topic. It can help you uncover questions you didn't even know you needed an answer for.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your stakeholder personally. The read on what they care about is a starting point — sanity-check it against what you actually know. And if you're already deep into a project, AI can't tell you what's politically charged or off-limits in your org. That's still your call.
steps: Pull together everything you already know about the project and the person. Old emails, the meeting invite, the kickoff doc, the org chart, whatever's lying around. The more you give the prompt, the less it has to guess.
Read the output as a draft, not a script. AI will land some sections and miss on others. Cut what feels generic. Sharpen what's specific to your situation.
Use section 2 (what you need to walk out with) as your meeting compass. If the conversation drifts away from those items, you have the questions to steer it back.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who has run hundreds of needs assessment calls.
prompt_fast.ask: Prep me for the needs assessment call described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — kickoff brief, project doc, prior emails, anything I've got. Messy is fine.]
Who I'm meeting with: [NAME, ROLE, what they care about, any prior interactions]
How much time we have: [e.g., 30 min, 1 hour]

Format the output as Markdown with these five sections:

1. **Context & stakeholder read** — A 5-bullet brief on the project state and what's likely on this person's mind. What they care about, what they're under pressure on.

2. **What I need to walk out with** — The concrete inputs this call has to produce: the gap, the audience, the constraints, the success criteria, what's been tried. Be specific to THIS project.

3. **Questions — both sides of the table** —
   - Questions to ask them. Each one tied to a specific item in section 2. No floating questions.
   - Questions they're likely to ask me. With a sentence of draft answer.

4. **Glossary** — Acronyms, internal product names, or domain terms from the input. Brief definitions so I don't nod blankly.

5. **If we only have 15 minutes** — The 2–3 questions I cannot leave without asking.

Don't:
- Ask questions that aren't tied to a takeaway in section 2.
- Invent context about the stakeholder. If you're inferring, flag it.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you had to guess at what this person cares about because I didn't give you enough input, call it out — those are sanity-check items, not facts. If my context doesn't match a needs assessment (e.g., we're already past discovery), flag it before answering.

pro_tip: Take the takeaways list from section 2 and turn it into a tight paragraph you send the person 24h before the meeting: "Here's where we are, here's what I'd love to get out of our conversation, here are two questions I'd appreciate you thinking about beforehand." Senior stakeholders will respect you more for it, and SMEs will come more prepared.

title: Build a Style Guide for your project
teaser: So your fourth module doesn't sound like a different person wrote it.
intro: Without a style guide, every piece of content you (or your team) writes sounds slightly different. The voice drifts, the vocabulary gets inconsistent, the naming conventions multiply. By the fourth module, you're not sure if you say "users" or "learners," and the character names have switched halves. The fix is to overtly define those choices up front so everyone — including the AI you'll prompt — stays on the same page.
why_matters: A style guide defines voice, vocabulary, examples, accessibility, and the small calls (contractions yes or no, second person or third, em-dash vs comma) that compound into inconsistency at scale. It's also what lets you scale beyond yourself — across SMEs, contractors, and AI runs.
how_ai_helps:
AI can do this in two ways:

  • From scratch. Tell it your audience, your modalities, and the tone you're going for. It drafts a complete, sectioned style guide — voice attributes, vocabulary, examples, conventions — ready for you to react to.
  • From existing content. Paste in your brand guide, a past course you loved, or any content with the voice you want. AI extrapolates the design decisions baked into it and turns them into explicit, reusable rules.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your brand the way someone who's lived in it does. The first draft is a 70% solution — it gets you off the blank page, but you (or someone who has) need to vet anything voice-or-tone related, plus what your organization considers off-limits. Treat AI as the drafter, not the decider.
steps: Gather your inputs. The big four: existing brand guidelines (if any), one piece of content you'd want this project to sound like, an audience description, and which modalities you'll produce.
Paste the prompt. Fill the brackets. The more you give it, the less it guesses.
Edit the output. AI gives you a starting point — you make the calls that don't match how you actually want to write.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior learning experience designer who has written style guides for dozens of projects across industries.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a style guide for the project described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO — role, expertise, motivation, context they'll read in]
Modalities you'll produce: [e.g., e-learning + job aids + facilitator notes]
Existing brand guidelines: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — voice attributes, banned words, tone rules, visual basics. If none yet, say "none yet."]
Sample content (optional, strong signal): [PASTE one piece you'd want this project to sound like]
Audience constraints (optional): [reading level, accessibility, languages, cultural considerations]

Format the output as Markdown so I can paste it into other prompts later.

Sections to include — but ONLY if I've given you input to ground them:
- Voice & tone (3–5 voice attributes; each with do/don't + one example)
- Vocabulary (terms to use, terms to avoid, terms to define on first use)
- Examples & analogies (what kinds work for THIS audience, what to avoid)
- Naming conventions (modules, characters, sections, files)
- Modality-specific rules (different per modality — slide rules ≠ video rules)
- Accessibility & inclusive language (concrete dos and don'ts for THIS audience)
- Visual treatment (only if visual specs were provided; otherwise skip)

Also add any other sections the input suggests are needed. If the source uses emoji, include a section on emoji. If it references regulated terms, include a section on regulated language. Use your judgment.

For every rule, include a concrete example. No generic advice.

Don't:
- Pad with sections I didn't ground in input.
- Use generic style-guide examples. Every example should be plausible for THIS project, with THIS audience, in THIS voice.
- Add "be authentic" or "be human" or "write clearly" without a concrete behavioral example.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you had to guess at the brand voice because I didn't give you enough input, flag it explicitly under each section. If the brand guidelines and the audience pull in opposite directions (e.g., very formal brand, very young audience), surface the tension as a question for me to answer, not a guess. If a section can't be grounded in input, skip it instead of padding.

pro_tip: Treat the style guide as living. After you've written 2–3 modules, audit them: where did you violate the guide? Either revise the modules to match, or update the guide to reflect the call you actually made. Drop the latest version into a Claude Project or custom GPT so every future authoring conversation references it automatically. The guide gets sharper as you go, and the project stays consistent without you policing it.

title: Turn a topic into a module outline
teaser: Because "I'll just start with the slides" never ends well.
intro: You've got a topic and some bullet points from a kickoff call. You're ready to create the course. Wait — don't do it yet. The first thing you should do is draft an outline.
why_matters: An outline is the ordered list of modules — each one with a stated learning outcome, a few key sub-topics, and a reason for sitting where it does. Get it wrong and you'll fight your way through development.
how_ai_helps:
AI is good at generating a defensible first-pass structure in seconds. The trick is constraining it so that it gives you what you need. The prompt below bakes in three constraints that turn 'AI gave me an outline' into 'AI gave me a usable outline':

  • Each module needs a stated learning outcome (not just a topic).
  • Each module needs a one-line rationale — so you know why it sits in that position.
  • The module count is capped — fights the AI urge to over-deliver.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your learners. After you have an outline, you still need to check it against what you learned in Analysis & Discovery.
steps: Collect your topic, audience description, and any source bullet points or documents. The more you give it up front, the better the first draft.
Paste the prompt into Claude or ChatGPT. Fill in the brackets.
Iterate. Ask for a different sequence, a different module count, a different cognitive progression. The first output is rarely the final outline.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a module-by-module outline for a training program.
prompt_fast.shape:
Topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO THE LEARNERS ARE — role, expertise level, motivation]
Format: [e.g., 60-minute live workshop / 4-hour async / 30-min microlearning]
Source material (optional): [BULLET POINTS OR PASTED SOURCE CONTENT]

Rules:
- 4 to 6 modules. Don't go over.
- Each module needs: a title, a stated learning outcome (use a Bloom-level verb), 3–5 sub-points, and a one-line rationale for why it sits in this position.
- Sequence the modules so each one enables the next.

Don't:
- Pad with filler modules to hit a number.
- Use vague verbs like "understand" or "learn about" — pick a Bloom verb that names a real behavior.
- Number the modules 1–N unless the order actually matters.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you assumed something, call it out — those become my SME questions. If the brief is too thin to design from, ask me one clarifying question first instead of guessing.

pro_tip: If you have actual source material — SOPs, interview transcripts, articles — drop it all into a NotebookLM notebook and run this prompt in there. The outline comes back grounded in your real sources, with citations. Way less hallucinated content, and much easier to defend to an SME.
